title: Go new Function
content:
### The new Function

`new(T)` allocates memory for a type T and returns a pointer to it.

### Basic Usage

```go
p := new(int)   // *int, initialized to 0
fmt.Println(*p) // 0
*p = 42
fmt.Println(*p) // 42
```

### new vs make

| Function | Works With | Returns | Memory |
|----------|------------|---------|--------|
| `new(T)` | Any type | `*T` (pointer) | Zeroed |
| `make(T)` | Slice, map, channel only | `T` (initialized) | Initialized |

### new with Structs

```go
type Person struct {
    Name string
    Age  int
}

p := new(Person)
fmt.Println(p.Name) // "" (zero value)
fmt.Println(p.Age)  // 0 (zero value)

p.Name = "Alice"
p.Age = 30
```

### new vs &T{}

```go
// These are equivalent:
p1 := new(Person)
p2 := &Person{}

// But &Person{Name: "Alice"} is NOT possible with new
p3 := &Person{Name: "Alice", Age: 30}
```

### new for Built-in Types

```go
i := new(int)
fmt.Println(*i) // 0

f := new(float64)
fmt.Println(*f) // 0

s := new(string)
fmt.Println(*s) // "" (empty)

b := new([3]int)
fmt.Println(*b) // [0, 0, 0]
```

### Idiomatic Usage

`new` is rarely used in Go. Most Go developers prefer:

```go
// Instead of new(int)
var x int

// Instead of new(MyStruct)
ms := MyStruct{}

// For pointers to initialized values
ms := &MyStruct{Field: "value"}
```

> **Key Point:** `new` is not commonly used in Go. The `&T{}` syntax is more idiomatic for structs, and `var x T` is preferred for zero-valued types.

quiz.question: Can new() be used with slices?
